feed.title: Combined summary - [Research] A clockless vardiff strands a slowing miner

entry.title: Combined summary - [Research] A clockless vardiff strands a slowing miner
entry.summary: The issue addressed in the description centers around the challenge faced by variable difficulty (vardiff) algorithms when a miner's operational pace significantly slows down but does not cease entirely. This slow-down can occur due to various reasons such as demand response actions, overheating mitigation, or intentional curtailment. The core problem arises when the hashrate of the miner decreases yet the mining difficulty remains high, as it was calibrated for a faster rate. This misalignment causes the miner to produce fewer shares than expected, which in turn diminishes the vardiff controller's ability to accurately gauge and adjust the mining difficulty. Vardiff functions as a feedback loop where the share rate acts as a sensor and mining difficulty as an actuator, with the target being the miner’s hashrate. Ideally, this system adjusts difficulty based on the incoming rate of shares to match the miner’s output capability. However, if the miner starts producing shares at a slower rate due to increased difficulty or decreased operational capacity, the feedback loop weakens. This is because the sensor (share rate) fails primarily when the difficulty is set too high relative to the miner’s hashrate, resulting in fewer shares and inadequate data for recalibrating the difficulty. Essentially, the controller becomes 'blind' at the very moment it needs to be most effective. The severity of this issue is quantifiable. The statistical variation inherent in counting shares means that as the share rate drops, the uncertainty in measuring the true rate increases exponentially. This relationship is encapsulated by the Cramér–Rao bound, establishing a lower limit to the accuracy of any estimate based on these shares. As shares become sparse, the controller's estimation error increases, stranding the miner at a difficulty level it cannot effectively operate at. To counteract this, the solution proposed involves allowing the vardiff controller to adjust difficulty based not just on share rates but also on time—a method known as feedforward control. This adjustment would operate independently of share input, incrementally easing the difficulty over a set period until shares begin to normalize, ensuring the miner is not left in a high-difficulty limbo that its reduced hashrate cannot sustain. Practical testing of different vardiff implementations can be conducted using a shaping proxy, allowing for controlled manipulation of perceived share rates without altering actual mining operations. This enables direct observation of how different pool algorithms respond to rapid changes in share submission rates, providing a clear measure of their robustness and responsiveness to real-world mining fluctuations. In essence, understanding and addressing the limitations of vardiff systems in dynamic mining environments is crucial. It ensures that miners remain productive and efficient, even as external conditions fluctuate, preventing scenarios where miners are left 'stranded' by overly optimistic difficulty settings that do not reflect their current operational realities.
